text:
Engineer Notebook

Note anything important you learned about stakeholder mapping, any questions you have, any initial design ideas or sketches you have or whatever else you want to write about!

You can write or sketch (or do both). You do not need to write in complete sentences.

Optionally, you can answer one (or both) of these questions in your journal:

  • What do you think you did well in today's lesson? What do you want to improve?
  • Explain what you think you should do next in your design process.

Note anything important you learned about stakeholder mapping, any questions you have, any initial design ideas or sketches you have or whatever else you want to write about!

You can write or sketch (or do both). You do not need to write in complete sentences.

Optionally, you can answer one (or both) of these questions in your journal:

  • What do you think you did well in today's lesson? What do you want to improve?
  • Explain what you think you should do next in your design process.
  • Today's Recap: Identifying Stakeholders
  • Today you identified several stakeholders who are impacted by water conservation in one or many ways. Remember, a stakeholder is anyone who cares about your design problem and solution, or will be directly or indreictly affected by it. This includes people who will use your solution, support it, fund it, or even oppose it. Understanding your stakeholders helps you design solution that work for your community.
Where did an AI help you update the need statement? Please describe.
Where did you find that AI was not helpful to you? Why? What type of support did you expect from AI to develop a Need Statement?

Documentation

Document your design process. Take your stakeholder map, need statement notes, and engineer notebook tile(s) and add them to the Learning Journal.
